result:
Lately I’ve been thinking about pendulums: how every mood I have seems secretly tethered to its opposite, swinging slowly, keeping cosmic time. A week ago I was flattened by a small disappointment—a missed email, a canceled plan—yet last night the same inbox chimed with an acceptance, and the room filled with champagne light. The distance between trough and crest can’t be more than an atom’s-width of perspective, but it feels continental. I wonder if the heart obeys some hidden conservation law: for every hour I feel small, another must be lent when I feel vast. The old Greeks carved both tragedy and comedy into the same mask; maybe they sensed that our interior metabolisms run on exchange rates we never notice.

Sometimes I sit beside the Capitol Crescent Trail, watching cyclists tear past like comets, and I try to breathe in proportion to their speed. Inhale: whole galaxies approach. Exhale: whole galaxies recede. When the schoolchildren march by with identical neon backpacks, I imagine each one carrying an undiscovered moon. The universe hands out satellites the way grandmothers hand out peppermints—generous, matter-of-fact, pocket-warmed. I like to think even my cheap gray hoodie gets heavier from collected starlight if I wear it long enough. Outside, the spring magnolias are arranging their bruise-colored petals like notes in a clandestine lullaby, and the city smells of wet asphalt and possibility. Somewhere a saxophone practices scales that climb, collapse, and climb again—pendulum—reminding me that music is only ever the sound of motion meeting memory, over and over.

Sometimes I sit beside the Capitol Crescent Trail, watching cyclists tear past like comets, and I try to breathe in proportion to their speed. Inhale: whole galaxies approach. prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
